items.title: Iraola Named Liverpool Manager After Slot's Departure
items.summary: Andoni Iraola has been appointed Liverpool manager following Arne Slot's surprise exit on 30 May.
items.why: The Reds are under entirely new management heading into 2026/27, with Salah, Robertson and Konate also gone.
items.readout: Liverpool have appointed Andoni Iraola as their new manager, with the Spaniard taking charge just three weeks ago after Arne Slot departed on the 30th of May. It marks a significant moment for the club, as this coming season will be the first since 2016/17 without Mohamed Salah, and the first without Andy Robertson and Ibrahima Konate following the trio's contract expiries. Iraola has wasted no time getting to work, and the summer will be a crucial period of rebuilding as he looks to put his own stamp on the squad. The next competitive action will be pre-season friendlies starting in late July.

items.title: Victor Munoz Signs Six-Year Deal at Anfield
items.summary: Spain international Victor Munoz has put pen to paper on a six-year contract, Iraola's first signing.
items.why: A long-term commitment signals Iraola's rebuilding ambitions after the departures of Salah and the senior defensive core.
items.readout: Liverpool have completed the signing of Victor Munoz, a Spain international, on a six-year deal, making him Andoni Iraola's first addition to the squad. The attacking player arrives as the club looks to address the significant gap left by Mohamed Salah's departure, with the manager keen to bring in quality options from the outset of his tenure. The length of the contract reflects the club's long-term vision under their new manager, and supporters will be hoping this is just the first of several arrivals before the season kicks off. The deal was confirmed earlier this week and Munoz is expected to report for pre-season in July.

items.title: Liverpool Receive Bradley Barcola Transfer Boost
items.summary: Liverpool are reportedly making concrete progress on a deal for PSG winger Bradley Barcola this summer.
items.why: Barcola would be a marquee addition as Liverpool reshape their attack under Iraola after Salah's exit.
items.readout: Liverpool have received a boost in their pursuit of PSG winger Bradley Barcola, with reports suggesting the Reds are making genuine progress on a deal. The French international is seen as a potential high-profile addition to a forward line that has lost Salah, and his arrival would represent a significant statement of intent from the new management. Liverpool are also monitoring Yan Diomande from RB Leipzig, though that club is holding firm on its valuation of around 130 million euros. Supporters will be hoping the club can get further signings tied up quickly before pre-season friendlies get under way at the end of next month.

items.title: Worcestershire Council Tax Rises by Nearly 9%
items.summary: Worcestershire County Council has raised council tax by 8.98%, adding £2.79 a week for Band D households.
items.why: A significant rise for local residents already feeling cost-of-living pressures, driven by soaring social care costs.
items.readout: Worcestershire County Council has raised council tax by 8.98% for 2026/27, meaning Band D households are now paying an extra £2.79 a week. The council says the increase has been driven by sharply rising demand for adult and children's social care services, compounded by higher costs from National Minimum Wage increases and inflation. The county is investing £193 million in capital projects this year, including road improvements and flood mitigation, but the financial pressures on the authority remain significant. Residents struggling with costs can contact the Worcestershire Advice Network for support with benefits, housing, and debt.

items.title: Powick Old Bridge Repair Works Continue
items.summary: Essential investigation works are ongoing at Powick Old Bridge as engineers seek a permanent repair solution.
items.why: The historic bridge remains a disruption for local drivers, and residents will want to know progress on a fix.
items.readout: Works on Powick Old Bridge continued this month as part of Worcestershire County Council's ongoing programme to find a permanent repair solution for the historic structure. Investigation work took place on the 10th of June, with engineers continuing to assess the options available. The bridge features as part of the council's broader £193 million capital investment programme for the county. No firm date has been set for a permanent fix, but the authority says it is committed to resolving the issue and updates will be shared as the programme progresses.

items.title: 13 Million in UK Living in Relative Poverty
items.summary: ONS figures show 13 million people in the UK were in relative poverty to March 2025, up half a million year-on-year.
items.why: The national picture reinforces local pressures on Worcestershire household budgets and demand for council services.
items.readout: Office for National Statistics figures show that 13 million people in the UK were living in relative poverty in the year to March 2025, an increase of half a million compared to the previous twelve months. The data paints a stark picture of the ongoing cost-of-living challenge facing households across the country, including in Worcestershire where the council has had to raise council tax significantly to meet rising demand for social care. The OECD forecasts UK economic growth of just 0.7% this year, in part due to the impact of the ongoing Iran conflict on energy costs and supply chains. Local support services, including the Worcestershire Advice Network, are available to help residents with benefits and debt advice.

items.title: Nintendo Switch 2 Sales Top 19.86 Million, Outselling PS5
items.summary: Switch 2 has sold nearly 20 million units in its first year, comfortably outselling the PS5 in the same quarter.
items.why: The Switch 2 is the dominant console on the market right now, shaping what publishers and developers prioritise.
items.readout: Nintendo Switch 2 has reached 19.86 million units sold in its fiscal year, overtaking the PS5 by around one million in the most recent quarter. That is a remarkable achievement for a console barely a year on from its June 2025 launch, and it places the Switch 2 as the clear market leader heading into the second half of 2026. Mario Kart World alone has shifted 14 million copies, while 13 new titles have released on the platform just this month. Publishers are clearly taking notice, with big multiplatform releases like Final Fantasy VII Rebirth and Devil May Cry 5 now arriving on Nintendo hardware for the first time.

items.title: Star Fox Returns on Switch 2 with New Title
items.summary: Nintendo's long-dormant Star Fox franchise made its comeback on 25 June with a new Switch 2 exclusive.
items.why: One of gaming's most beloved dormant franchises is back, giving Switch 2 owners another major exclusive.
items.readout: Nintendo has revived the Star Fox franchise with a brand new game released on the 25th of June for the Switch 2, ending years of silence for one of gaming's best-loved series. The game brings back the signature fast-paced space combat and aerial action that made the original titles so popular, and it arrives as part of a busy June for Nintendo software. The Switch 2 hardware has created a platform capable of hosting far more ambitious titles than its predecessor, and Star Fox's return signals Nintendo's intent to bring back classic franchises rather than relying solely on Mario and Zelda. It is available now in both digital and physical formats.

items.title: Summer Game Fest Confirms Final FF7 Remake Chapter and Resi Code Veronica
items.summary: Summer Game Fest reveals include the final FF7 Remake entry and a full Resident Evil Code Veronica remake.
items.why: Two of gaming's biggest franchises are getting major new entries, with wide appeal for PlayStation, Xbox and PC players.
items.readout: Summer Game Fest 2026 delivered some of the most anticipated announcements in recent memory, with confirmation of the concluding chapter in Square Enix's Final Fantasy VII Remake trilogy and a full remake of Resident Evil Code Veronica from Capcom. The FF7 project has been one of gaming's longest-running ongoing sagas, so news of its conclusion will be a significant moment for JRPG fans who have followed it since the first part released in 2020. The Code Veronica remake follows the acclaimed remakes of Resident Evil 2, 3, and 4, and is expected to retain the over-the-shoulder gameplay that won those titles such critical praise. No UK release dates have been confirmed yet, but both games are now officially in development.

items.title: ZEV Mandate Requires 33% of New Cars to Be Electric in 2026
items.summary: Car dealers must ensure a third of new car sales are fully electric under the UK's ZEV mandate for 2026.
items.why: The tightening target affects which models dealers push hardest, and could mean better EV deals for buyers.
items.readout: The UK's Zero Emission Vehicle mandate has stepped up in 2026, requiring that 33% of all new cars sold must be fully electric. That is a notable jump from the 22% target that applied two years ago, and dealers who miss it face financial penalties. The industry broadly met the earlier targets by using flexible compliance mechanisms, but the rising threshold means manufacturers are under increasing pressure to push EV sales. For buyers, this dynamic could result in better deals and incentives as dealers work to hit their numbers. The mandate is set to rise further to 80% by 2030, with a formal government review planned no later than early 2027.

items.title: Home EV Charger Grant Rises to £500 Per Socket
items.summary: The government's home charger grant for renters, landlords and businesses rose to £500 per socket from April 2026.
items.why: The higher grant cuts upfront installation costs, making home charging more accessible for prospective EV buyers.
items.readout: From April 2026, the government increased the home and workplace EV charger grant to £500 per socket, up from £350 previously, with the scheme extended until March 2027. The change applies to renters, landlords and businesses looking to install charging points, not just homeowners. Home charging remains the most cost-effective way to run an electric vehicle in the UK, with off-peak tariffs as low as 6.7 pence per kilowatt hour, meaning running costs of just 3 to 5 pence per mile compared to 17 to 23 pence for a petrol equivalent. If you have been putting off getting a home charger installed, the improved grant makes this a good moment to act.

items.title: Stable EV Policy Could Add £15.5bn to UK Economy
items.summary: A study finds a consistent ZEV mandate would generate £15.5 billion in economic benefits and thousands of jobs.
items.why: Policy certainty matters for UK EV investment and consumer confidence, and this research makes the case clearly.
items.readout: A new analysis published this month estimates that maintaining a stable and predictable Zero Emission Vehicle mandate could contribute £15.5 billion to the UK economy, while supporting the growth of a domestic charging and manufacturing ecosystem. The report arrives as the government's Cost of Charging Review is due to report in the third quarter of 2026, examining why public rapid charging remains expensive relative to home rates. There are currently 121,262 public chargers across the UK, with ultra-rapid chargers making up 11% of that total. Supporters of the EV transition argue that policy certainty rather than last-minute rule changes is the single most important factor in encouraging both investment and consumer confidence.

items.title: FTSE 100 Slips 0.69% as UK Lags Global Rally
items.summary: The FTSE 100 dipped 0.69% this week at around 10,530, bucking a broadly positive global equity trend.
items.why: UK equities underperformed as sterling strength and Iran war uncertainty weighed on large-cap exporters.
items.readout: The FTSE 100 slipped 0.69% this week, falling against the grain of a more positive period for global equities, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq both advancing. The index is trading around 10,530 points, near the top of its recent range but unable to break convincingly higher. Sterling's relative strength against the euro, following news of Chancellor Reeves' backing of Andy Burnham as Labour leader, has added a modest headwind for large-cap FTSE exporters whose overseas earnings are worth less when converted back to pounds. The ongoing impact of Middle East tensions on energy costs and supply chains continues to be a factor in UK stocks lagging their US counterparts.

items.title: S&P 500 Flat as Nasdaq Leads on Tech Strength
items.summary: The S&P 500 is essentially flat at 7,354, while the Nasdaq outperformed with a 2.43% advance this week.
items.why: US tech is driving gains even as the Fed signals rates will stay higher for longer after its latest meeting.
items.readout: The S&P 500 ended broadly flat on the week at 7,354 points, down just 0.05%, while the Nasdaq Composite put in a much stronger showing with a gain of 2.43%, led by technology stocks. The Federal Reserve's latest meeting sent short-term US Treasury yields sharply higher, with the two-year note hitting its highest level in over a year, suggesting markets are pricing in a more cautious path for rate cuts. Investment-grade corporate bonds generated negative returns, while high-yield bonds outperformed in the broader risk-on environment. For a sterling-based investor, the relative dollar strength means some of those overseas US equity gains are partially offset when converted back to pounds.

items.title: Bank of England Holds Rate at 3.75%
items.summary: The MPC voted 7-2 to hold Bank Rate at 3.75%, with two members pushing for a rise to 4%.
items.why: No cut this month, and the hawkish minority signals the BoE remains cautious with services inflation sticky at 3.7%.
items.readout: The Bank of England's Monetary Policy Committee voted seven to two to hold Bank Rate at 3.75% at its June meeting, with the two dissenters arguing for an increase to 4%. UK CPI inflation has eased to 2.8% overall, but services inflation remains sticky at 3.7%, giving the committee reason to hold rather than cut. The Bank noted some further softening in the labour market and signs of demand weakness, but the uncertain global backdrop, including the ongoing impact of the Iran conflict on energy prices, has kept the MPC cautious. Markets should not bank on a rate cut at the next decision in August.

items.title: Iran-US Conflict Escalates with Hormuz Tanker Strike
items.summary: A tanker was struck in the Strait of Hormuz on 27 June, with Iran saying it targeted US sites in retaliation.
items.why: Escalation in the world's most critical oil shipping lane has direct implications for energy prices and the UK economy.
items.readout: Tensions in the Gulf escalated sharply on the 27th of June after UK Maritime Trade Operations reported that a projectile struck a tanker in the Strait of Hormuz, damaging the vessel's bridge. Iran subsequently said it had targeted American military sites in the Gulf region in response to the attack. The incident comes just ten days after a memorandum of understanding intended to move the conflict toward a formal end was signed by the presidents of the United States and Iran, raising serious concerns about the durability of that agreement. The Iran conflict has hit the UK economy harder than any other G7 nation, with the OECD forecasting UK growth of just 0.7% for this year.

items.title: Reeves Backs Burnham as Labour Leader Amid Starmer Poll Collapse
items.summary: Chancellor Rachel Reeves publicly endorsed Andy Burnham as Labour's next leader on 25 June.
items.why: A Burnham succession would keep current fiscal policy intact, with implications for taxes, spending and rate expectations.
items.readout: Chancellor Rachel Reeves gave her public backing to Greater Manchester Mayor Andy Burnham as the next Labour leader on the 25th of June, urging him to continue with her economic programme, which she said was beginning to bear fruit. The endorsement comes amid a significant collapse in Prime Minister Keir Starmer's opinion poll ratings, with the populist party Reform making substantial political gains on the back of the immigration debate. Against this backdrop, the pound has risen to its best level against the euro in almost a year, as currency markets appear to welcome the prospect of policy continuity under Burnham. The leadership contest is expected to develop further over the coming weeks.

items.title: Israel-Lebanon Framework Signed but Hezbollah Rejects Deal
items.summary: Israel and Lebanon signed a US-brokered ceasefire framework, but Hezbollah's leader has rejected it outright.
items.why: The deal's survival is in doubt, and ongoing Middle East instability matters for UK travellers, energy prices and markets.
items.readout: Israel and Lebanon signed a US-brokered framework agreement on 26 June that would see Israeli forces begin withdrawing from parts of southern Lebanon. However, Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem rejected the agreement the following day, saying it should be replaced by the broader Iran-US memorandum of understanding. An Israeli drone strike on the Nabatieh area in southern Lebanon was also reported on the 27th, casting further doubt over the ceasefire's durability. The situation remains highly fluid, and UK travellers with plans to visit Lebanon or the wider region should check the Foreign Office's latest travel advice before making any arrangements.

podcast.text: [warm] Good morning and welcome to The Wire. It is Saturday the 27th of June, and there is plenty to get across today. We have a sharp escalation in the Gulf, a political shake-up at home, a new era at Anfield, and some genuinely big news for gamers. Let us get into it.

[measured] Starting with the most significant international story of the morning. A tanker has been struck in the Strait of Hormuz, and Iran is saying it has targeted American military sites in the Gulf region in retaliation. This comes just ten days after the US and Iran signed a memorandum of understanding that was supposed to be moving both sides toward a ceasefire. The deal already looks fragile.

[measured] And you can see that worry reflected in the markets. The FTSE 100 is down about 0.7% on the week, sitting around 10,530, while US and global equities have had a more positive run. The Iran situation adds uncertainty to energy prices, and that is weighing on UK stocks more than most. The OECD has the UK growing at just 0.7% this year, partly because the conflict is hitting us harder than any other G7 nation.

The Bank of England also met this month and voted seven to two to hold Bank Rate at 3.75%. Two members actually wanted to raise rates. With services inflation still running at 3.7%, the committee is not in any rush to cut. Do not pencil in a reduction at August's meeting just yet.

[thoughtful] On the domestic political front, Chancellor Rachel Reeves has publicly backed Andy Burnham to be the next Labour leader, and it seems to be landing well with markets. The pound is at its best against the euro in nearly a year. Starmer's poll ratings have taken a real beating, with Reform making a lot of the political weather on immigration. Burnham continuity seems to be what investors are hoping for.

[measured] And briefly on the Middle East, Israel and Lebanon did sign a US-brokered framework deal on the 26th, which looked like progress. But Hezbollah's leader rejected it the very next day. There was also a reported drone strike in southern Lebanon this morning. If you have travel plans to the region, check the Foreign Office guidance before you book anything.

[warm] Closer to home now, and Worcestershire residents will have noticed the council tax bill going up by nearly 9% this year. That is an extra £2.79 a week for a Band D household. The council says it is down to rising social care costs, and they are not wrong, but it is a tough increase for people already feeling the squeeze.

And on the roads front, work is still ongoing at Powick Old Bridge. Engineers are still looking at options for a permanent repair. The council has committed 193 million pounds to capital projects across the county this year, so the bridge is on the list. Just worth bearing in mind if it is on your usual route.

[excited] Right, let us get to Anfield. A new era is very much under way. Andoni Iraola is the new Liverpool manager, appointed three weeks ago after Arne Slot left at the end of May. And crucially, he has already made his first signing, bringing in Spain international Victor Munoz on a six-year deal. Six years. He is not hanging about.

There is also some encouraging transfer news on Bradley Barcola, with reports suggesting Liverpool are making real progress on a deal for the PSG winger. The squad looks very different this season without Salah, Robertson and Konate, so the window is absolutely critical. The first pre-season friendly is away to Sunderland on the 25th of July.

[excited] Gaming news now, and it has been a genuinely big week. Nintendo Switch 2 has hit 19.86 million units sold in its first year, which actually means it has outsold the PS5 in the most recent quarter. Just over a year since launch and it is already the dominant console on the market. Remarkable stuff.

[laughs] And Nintendo celebrated the milestone in style with the return of Star Fox on Wednesday. The franchise had been dormant for years and it is finally back, with fast-paced space combat exactly as fans remember it. If you have a Switch 2, it is out now.

[measured] Summer Game Fest also had some real announcements. The concluding chapter of the Final Fantasy VII Remake trilogy has been confirmed, which will be a big deal for JRPG fans who have followed the series since 2020. And Capcom is making a full remake of Resident Evil Code Veronica. No UK dates yet on either, but both are officially happening.

[thoughtful] A couple of useful things to know on the EV front this week. The ZEV mandate means car dealers legally have to ensure 33% of their new car sales are electric in 2026. That is up from 22% just two years ago, which means dealers have strong incentives to push EVs, and that could mean better deals for buyers shopping right now.

Also, if you have been thinking about a home charger, the government grant has been increased to 500 pounds per socket, up from 350, and the scheme runs until March 2027. Home charging works out at just 3 to 5 pence per mile on a good tariff, compared to 17 to 23 pence for petrol. The numbers still stack up well.

[warm] That is The Wire for this Saturday morning. A lot happening across the desks today, from the Strait of Hormuz to Anfield to your council tax bill. Have a great weekend, stay informed, and we will see you next time.
